headlines.title: Controversy surrounding Redwood City teacher's past
headlines.description: Families at a Redwood City middle school are upset and demanding action after it was revealed a current teacher was once fired from another California school district for reports of unwanted touching involving female students. Robert Handa has more about the controversy and what parents are demanding. Watch his report in the video above. Redwood City May 11 Redwood City Apr 21 Redwood City Apr 29 Redwood City judge denies Scott Peterson's request to present new evidence

headlines.title: 2026 FIFA World Cup: Bay Area schedule, watch parties, what to know
headlines.description: The Bay Area is inching closer to hosting FIFA World Cup 2026 matches. From a schedule of local matches to a list of watch parties across the region, here’s what to know ahead of the action. Scroll down to get information on the following sections: World Cup matches in the Bay Area What to know about the 2026 FIFA World Cup 2026 FIFA World Cup matches by host city What group is the U.S. men’s national team in? When will the U.S. men’s national team play? Where to watch the FIFA World Cup in the Bay Area How to get to Levi’s Stadium Security at the World Cup World Cup matches at Levi’s Stadium June 13, 12:00 p.m. Qatar vs. Switzerland – Group B June 16, 9:00 p.m. Austria vs. Jordan – Group J June 19, 9:00 p.m. Playoff winner of (Turkey, Romania, Slovakia or Kosovo) vs. Paraguay – Group D June 22, 8:00 p.m. Jordan vs. Algeria – Group J June 25, 7:00 p.m. Paraguay vs. Australia – Group D July 1, 5:00 p.m. Round of 32 match: Group D winner and 3rd place team from group B, E, F, I or J. 🗓️ Download PDF of FIFA’s full World Cup calendar by city here Complete Spanish coverage of the FIFA World Cup 2026 will air exclusively on Telemundo and Peacock. What to know about the 2026 FIFA World Cup There will be 104 games in this expanded World Cup running from June 11 through July 19 at 16 venues throughout North America. Seventy-eight games will be at 11 NFL stadiums, including all from the quarterfinals on, and 13 each in Mexico and Canada. The final is at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. In addition to 12 group winners and second-place nations, eight third-place teams advance to a new round of 32. The World Cup winner will play eight games. 2026 FIFA World Cup matches by host city Here is a breakdown of all 16 World Cup stadiums across the U.S., Mexico and Canada. United States Atlanta: 8 matches (including semifinal) Boston: 7 matches (including quarterfinal) Dallas: 9 matches (including semifinal) Houston: 7 matches Kansas City: 6 matches (including quarterfinal) Los Angeles: 8 matches (including quarterfinal) Miami: 7 matches (including quarterfinal and Bronze Final) New York/New Jersey: 8 matches (including World Cup Final) Philadelphia: 6 matches San Francisco/Bay Area: 6 matches Seattle: 6 matches Mexico Guadalajara: 4 matches Mexico City: 5 matches Monterrey: 4 matches Canada Toronto: 6 matches Vancouver: 7 matches 2026 World Cup May 8 USMNT May 11 FIFA World Cup May 11 What group is the U.S. men’s national team in? The U.S. men’s national team is in Group D with Australia, Paraguay and the winner of a European playoff between Turkey, Romania, Slovakia and Kosovo. When will the U.S. men’s national team play? Match 1: Friday, June 12 – USMNT vs. Paraguay, Los Angeles Stadium Match 2: Friday, June 19 – USMNT vs. Australia, Seattle Stadium Match 3: Thursday, June 25 – USMNT vs. Winner UEFA Playoff C, Los Angeles Stadium Where to watch the FIFA World Cup in the Bay Area If you can’t attend the 2026 FIFA World Cup games in person at Levi’s Stadium this summer, the Bay Area Host Committee just announced another way to enjoy the games for free. The BAHC will host free watch parties and fan zone locations across the Bay Area for the games. Beginning on June 11, the events will take place at more than 30 venues across the Bay Area. Here’s a list of the various participating venues and sports bars where fans can watch the games across the Bay Area: San Francisco Thrive City at Chase Center China Basin Park at Mission Rock (hosted by Giants Enterprises) PIER 39 Yerba Buena Lane (hosted by Pride House) The Midway (hosted by Visa) The Crossing at East Cut (hosted by Pride House) East Bay Alameda County Fair Grounds, Pleasanton Raimondi Park, Oakland (hosted by the Oakland Sports Group & Oakland Ballers) Downtown Oakland Hoffmann Theater, Lesher Center for the Arts, Walnut Creek SS Red Oak Victory Historic Ship, Richmond East Brother Beer Company, Richmond South Bay San Jose Earthquakes Soccer Celebration at San Pedro Square, San Jose Opening Weekend presented by the Bay Area Host Committee and SJ26 The Row Cup, Santana Row, San Jose Civic Center Plaza, Milpitas Milpitas Community Center Morgan Hill Cityline Redwood Square, Sunnyvale North Bay Sausalito Napa Valley Santa Rosa The Peninsula Courthouse Square, Redwood City Central Park, San Mateo Wheeler Parking Plaza, San Carlos Half Moon Bay Burlingame Mountain View Santa Cruz Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk Santa Cruz Museum of Art & History How to get to Levi’s Stadium If you’re headed to catch a game, here’s a breakdown on how to get to the stadium. VTA Fans in the South Bay can hop on a VTA light-rail train and head toward Great America station. They can also jump on a VTA bus and get dropped off near the stadium. Riders can also map out a visit by using VTA’s trip planner. Caltrain Fans living farther away from Santa Clara can hop on Caltrain, get off at the Mountain View station and transfer to VTA in order to get to the stadium. View Caltrain’s Levi’s Stadium page for more information on game day service. BART Fans can also jump on BART, get off at the Milpitas station and then transfer to the VTA orange line, which serves Levi’s Stadium. More details on the transit agency’s “Take BART to the game” page. ACE Fans coming from afar can take an ACE train all the way to the Great America station, which is just a quick walk away from the stadium. View ACE’s event train page for more information. Drive If you’re planning on driving to the game, visit LevisStadium.com for more information on directions and parking policies. Security at the World Cup The Santa Clara Police Department is the lead agency for security coordination during the World Cup matches. From rogue drones to cyberattacks, the Bay Area is preparing for heightened security risks as matches come to town. Senior Investigative Reporter Candice Nguyen takes a closer look at how law enforcement agencies are preparing for the World Cup. Watch her investigation below. The Associated Press contributed to this report.

headlines.title: San Leandro police chief charged with misdemeanor hit and run
headlines.description: The Alameda County District Attorney’s Office charged San Leandro’s police chief with misdemeanor hit and run in connection with a 2025 collision, the office announced Wednesday. The charge against Chief Angela Averiett stems from a collision that happened at about 10:51 p.m. on May 19, 2025, on eastbound Interstate 580 near the Interstate 680 interchange, according to the district attorney’s office. The district attorney’s office said it became aware of the case on April 1 of this year. After receiving the California Highway Patrol’s investigation report and conducting additional investigative work, the district attorney found sufficient probable cause to formally file the hit-and-run charge. “Our office has a duty to independently review the evidence and pursue charges when the facts support criminal charges,” District Attorney Ursula Jones Dickson said in a statement. The maximum punishment is six months in county jail and a $1,000 fine, according to the district attorney’s office. Averiett’s arraignment has been set for June 18.

headlines.title: Fremont police investigate possible shooting
headlines.description: Police said they are investigating a possible shooting that left at least one person hurt in Fremont on Wednesday afternoon. According to authorities, multiple callers described a group of teenagers fighting around Corrigan Drive and Eggers Drive before a possible shooting at around 6:17 p.m. Initially, officers did not find any victims at the scene; the victim was found later at his home, according to police. The police department added officers also identified a suspect vehicle driving away the area through witness interview and their Real Time Information Center. Authorities said this is an active investigation.

headlines.title: San Jose police investigating after child hospitalized with life-threatening injuries
headlines.description: A child was taken to a hospital with life-threatening injuries following an incident Wednesday in East San Jose, authorities said. Emergency crews responded to the incident along the 1200 block of Lancelot Lane just before 10 a.m. Police said an investigation is active and ongoing. No other information was immediately available. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. San Jose May 12 San Jose 10 hours ago San Jose May 10 Golden Mussels discovered at water treatment plants in San Jose

headlines.title: Deadline approaching to claim funds under the SF City Option program
headlines.description: A deadline is approaching for workers to collect money they are owed under the SF City Option, a controversial city-mandated health insurance program. The program was called a model for low-cost health care. It was instituted years ago to help uninsured employees at various San Francisco businesses afford healthcare. Those who qualified were given an account, but also a deadline, for using those funds. “Unless action is taken by May 21, 2026, any SF City Option funds or accounts that have been inactive for at least three consecutive years will be permanently closed and those funds will no longer be available,” said Maureen Kelly with the Department of Public Health. Kelly says the agency has been doing a lot of outreach to get the word out about The San Francisco City Option. “Helps people who work in San Francisco cover their medical bills, dental bills, prescription glasses, baby formula, massages and more,” she said. About $1 billion in healthcare expenses have been reimbursed since the program began in 2008. “To keep your account active, do one quick step before May 21: You can call the San Francisco City Option Customer Service Center and confirm your information, submit and enrollment form, or if you’re already enrolled, submit a claim,” she said. City Option is also one way San Francisco employers with 20 or more workers can comply with a mandate to provide insurance for their employees, but it means they pay into the program. Now, some of those businesses want to know why forfeited funds would go back to the city and not the employers who paid into the program. Others say they have an issue with the program outright. “We cannot have more than 18 employees because then we would be forced to pay this and we can’t afford it, it would bankrupt us, the problem is that the money is supposed to be put into a healthcare savings account, which it is for over 19 employees, and then nobody uses it,” said Ben Bleiman, who runs Harrington’s Bar and Grill. One estimate suggests more than $200 million in unclaimed funds could be forfeited at the end of the month, but the city says there’s still time for eligible employees to make a claim. The Golden Gate Restaurant Association said in a statement the ordinance is flawed and requires reform adding: “We remain actively engaged with city leaders and stakeholders to develop a solution that preserves employee access to healthcare and supports long term viability of San Francisco’s small business community.” “This has been an issue we have been working on for the past year with our partners with our small business community with labor and we continue to have conversations. We want to make it easier for small business owners to run their business here in San Francisco, and we have to refrom this law that is now 20 years old and we also want to look out for our workers so there is a deal to be had there,” Mayor Daniel Lurie said.

headlines.title: Recology cracks down on overflowing trash cans in San Mateo County
headlines.description: Recology, which provides trash services to much of the Bay Area, started taking photos of residential trash cans in San Mateo County. The pictures are snapped automatically from the company’s trucks as the containers are emptied. Employees then examine the photos and send warning notices to people about violations like overfilled trash cans. If residents are caught doing it a third time, the company says they could face additional charges. “I haven’t got notifications that they were doing that, it would’ve been nice to let us know that that’s what they are doing,” said Nicole Rodriguez, a Recology customer. A Recology spokesman says so far only a small percentage of residents have overstuffed their bins. The company has already been taking pictures of commercial bins in San Mateo County for the past two years. Recology says it’s trying to prevent garbage from spilling onto sidewalks and make sure customers who have more trash use bigger bins.

headlines.title: Officials say $1.3 billion in Medicaid money to California will be deferred over suspicions of fraud
headlines.description: Vice President JD Vance on Wednesday announced new steps in the Trump administration’s initiative to root out fraud in federal health programs, including a $1.3 billion deferral in Medicaid funding to California. “How long are people going to pay into programs if they know that that money doesn’t go to a low-income kid who needs healthcare, but that money goes into a fraudster getting rich?” Vance said during an event at the White House, adding that taxpayers and program beneficiaries are victimized by such fraud. The Republican administration also is imposing a six-month freeze on some new Medicare enrollments and warning states to investigate Medicaid fraud or risk losing funding, officials said. The moves are part of Vance’s anti-fraud task force, which has been taking more aggressive steps to investigate states before the November elections. The panel set up by President Donald Trump seeks to crack down on potential misuse of public money. Vance, a potential 2028 White House hopeful, has used the high-profile assignment from Trump to remind Americans struggling with high costs that he is trying to claw back taxpayer dollars. Vance has promoted the task force’s work during campaign stops for Republican candidates and is expected to focus on the effort Thursday in Maine, which has closely watched primary races scheduled for June 9. The steps come as people across the United States have raised concerns about rising health costs and barriers to access, sometimes from the federal government’s own actions. New work requirements in Medicaid, for example, are expected to strain hospitals around the country and result in millions of enrollees losing their health coverage. The administration contends its vigorous fraud-busting efforts will help prevent wrongdoing in Medicaid and Medicare while preserving funding and resources for those most in need. Deferring $1.3 billion in California payments Dr. Mehmet Oz, who leads the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, said the administration was making the “largest deferral we’ve ever made” in Medicaid funds and that it was justified. He claimed the administration had identified questionable expenditures and anomalies, such as a higher rate of growth in California’s home care program compared with other states. He did not provide concrete examples of documented fraud. “We’d like the state to at least come to the table and explain to us how these outlier payments have been generated,” he said. The press office of Gov. Gavin Newsom, D-Calif., disputed Oz’s claims and said the state’s home care program grew because the state is “keeping more people OUT of far more expensive nursing homes.” “We hate fraud,” the office wrote on X. “But that’s NOT what this is.” The total cost of California’s Medicaid program, including state and federal funding, is expected to be about $222 billion for the budget year that starts July 1. Nationwide freeze on some new Medicare provider enrollments Oz’s agency also announced a nationwide six-month moratorium on all new Medicare enrollments by providers of hospice and home care. “Today we’re shutting the door on fraud — preventing new bad actors from entering Medicare while we aggressively identify, investigate, and remove those already exploiting them,” he said in a statement. Existing hospice and home healthcare providers will continue to operate as usual. But CMS said it will “intensify targeted investigations, deploy advanced data analytics, and accelerate the removal” of providers in the category that are suspected of fraudulent activity. Such a freeze is not unprecedented, said Tricia Neumann, a senior vice president and executive director for the program on Medicare policy at the healthcare research nonprofit KFF. She said President Bill Clinton’s Democratic administration also imposed a temporary moratorium on home health agencies. “A brief moratorium gives the administration time to crack down on true fraud and prevent new fraudulent entities from popping up,” she said. Several alleged fraud schemes have been prosecuted in the hospice and home healthcare categories, and states have acknowledged that it is a legitimate concern. But some have pushed back on the administration’s aggressive tactics and raised concerns that the catchall efforts could needlessly punish law-abiding providers that are trying to serve patients. The country’s largest organization advocating for home healthcare providers, the National Alliance for Care at Home, said in a statement that it supports efforts to root out fraud. But it said it prefers targeted strategies to a sweeping moratorium, which it said raises concerns about access to care as well as reduces competition and slows innovation. Also Wednesday, the Department of Health and Human Services’ internal watchdog sent letters to state attorneys general warning them to vigorously investigate possible fraud or risk losing federal money. Moves are part of monthslong federal push In recent months, CMS has suspended payments to hundreds of hospice and home care agencies in Los Angeles over alleged fraud and issued another six-month moratorium on suppliers of durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics and certain other supplies in Medicare. The administration also has approached at least five states with investigations into potential healthcare fraud and halted some $243 million in Medicaid payments to Minnesota over fraud concerns. Last month, Oz announced CMS would add to that oversight by requiring all 50 states to share how they planned to revalidate some of their Medicaid providers. In at least one case, the administration has erred in its accusations against states. In April, CMS acknowledged to The Associated Press that it made a significant error in figures it used to help justify a fraud probe in New York. The acknowledgment deepened doubts about the administration’s methods and raised a common criticism of the second Trump administration — that it tends to attack first and confirm the facts later. ___ Swenson reported from New York. Associated Press writers Tran Nguyen in Sacramento, Geoff Mulvihill in Haddonfield, New Jersey, Patrick Whittle in Portland, Maine, and Joey Cappelletti contributed to this report.
headlines.source: Ali Swenson | The Associated Press and Michelle L. Price | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Future of longtime Oakland martial arts studio up in the air
headlines.description: After 46 years in the same San Pablo Avenue storefront, the future of Oakland’s venerable Hand to Hand Kajukenbo Self Defense Center is up in the air like a roundhouse kick. The martial arts studio, which opened in 1980 to train women and the LGBTQ community, was recently informed by its building owner he planned to sell the building and they would have to vacate their longtime studio. As the group began searching and fundraising for a new location, the owner relented and offered a two-year lease extension — albeit with higher rent. It’s left the studio’s leaders mulling over what’s the next move: stay or find a new home. “We’re closing down a chapter that’s been so very meaningful,” said lead instructor Professor Sonya Richardson before learning of the potential lease extension. “We are the school, but this is our home.” As students of varying degrees and belts gathered for evening classes during a recent day, there was a sense of melancholy as well as excitement over a potential change of location. “It’s like losing a little piece of yourself,” said student Ana Sheriff. “Like physically, like an arm, a body, a piece of your heart a little bit.” Students at Oakland’s Hand to Hand Kajukenbo Self Defense Center perform kicks during a recent class. Though the upheaval had shaken up the school, Richardson, who’s served as lead instructor the last 24 years, had nothing but praise for the building’s owner, who she said long offered below-market rent. “Our building owner has been an amazing person,” Richardson said. “I feel like he has had such an act of love with us over 46 years.” The studio was founded by the late Professor Coleen Gragen with a goal of teaching self-defense skills to girls and women. The school teaches Kajukenbo, a hybrid of five different styles of martial arts. Hand to Hand’s legacy of inclusivity expanded over the years to become a welcoming space for the LGBTQ community. “I feel like the day I walked in the door I looked at people of all colors and shapes and backgrounds,” said student Lex Martin. “And so many kinds of people doing something together.” The leader of the studio said it isn’t just a self-defense school, but a community within itself. “We’ve had so many students come through and say, ‘This is where I feel most safe, I can be myself, I can express myself here,’” said seventh-degree black belt and assistant lead instructor Aarin Burch. Students at Oakland’s Hand to Hand Kajukenbo Self Defense Center perform kicks during a recent class. With the future unsettled, Richardson felt waves of nostalgia as she looked around the studio she’s been a part of for 30 years — the creak of the highly-sheened wood floors, the art on the walls, the altar created by a student, and the sign bearing the studio’s mantra: ‘The more sweat in peace, the less blood in war.’ “I love just pausing even in these days of feeling some loss,” Richardson said. “It really gives me a chance to pause and appreciate who we are, who we’ve been.” Wherever the school goes next, if it moves at all, Richardson said it was important to focus on the fact that Hand to Hand was ultimately about the people, not a building or even a space. Just like the act of facing an opponent is about applying lessons but focusing on the present. “We have to let everything go in the movement – the wins, the losses, the benefits, the fear – and just stay right there with the person who’s right in front of you,” she said.

headlines.title: Man charged with vehicular manslaughter in deadly collision in SF's Chinatown
headlines.description: A 76-year-old man has been charged with vehicular manslaughter and other offenses for a collision that killed a pedestrian and injured another in San Francisco’s Chinatown neighborhood back in March, prosecutors announced Wednesday. Zhuo Ming Lu pleaded not guilty Tuesday to all charges and allegations in connection with the March 27 collision in the area of Grant Avenue and Jackson Street. The victim who died has been identified as Cutberto Zamora, also known as “Beto,” according to a GoFundMe fundraising campaign created to support his family. The GoFundMe account described him as “an amazing father who was always working hard to provide a beautiful life for his family.” A preliminary investigation by police determined that Lu was trying to park his vehicle when he hit the two pedestrians on the sidewalk and then struck a building, according to the San Francisco District Attorney’s Office. Lu stayed at the scene and cooperated with investigators, but was arrested in April and was charged with misdemeanor vehicular manslaughter while driving a vehicle in the commission of unlawful acts, as well as driving at an unsafe speed, prosecutors said. A judge ordered him released on his own recognizance on the conditions that he not drive, and must surrender his driver’s license and passport. He is set to return to court on Sept. 30 for a pre-trial hearing. San Francisco Apr 17 San Francisco Mar 30 Deadly San Francisco Chinatown crash: Family mourns, pedestrian safety addressed

headlines.title: OpenAI trial: Testimony wraps up, closing arguments set for Thursday
headlines.description: What to Know Elon Musk filed a $134 billion lawsuit against Sam Altman and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft, over its investments in OpenAI, in 2024. Musk claims OpenAI violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. OpenAI has brushed off Musk’s allegations as a case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. The nine-person jury was selected on April 27. The trial is being held in Oakland federal court and scheduled to continue through late May. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella spent about three hours on the stand Monday, May 10, with his testimony diving into Microsoft’s investment in OpenAI and his perspective on Altman’s firing. Altman’s testimony was heard on Tuesday, May 12. Closing arguments are expected to start Thursday, May 14. Editor’s note: This live blog has ended and is no longer being updated. NBC Bay Area’s Sergio Quintana is at the federal courthouse in Oakland and providing live updates below.
headlines.source: Sergio Quintana, Kristofer Noceda and Brendan Weber

headlines.title: New falcon chicks at San Jose City Hall doing well
headlines.description: The four newly-hatched falcon chicks that call San Jose City Hall home are healthy, according to biologists. Biologists from UC Santa Cruz determined all four little birds of prey are male and placed bands on them in order to track them. In addition to the biologists, volunteers also have eyes on the chicks to make sure they don’t come down from their city hall nest before it’s time. The chicks will have enough feathers and wing strength to fly at about six weeks. The parents will continue to feed them and protect them until they learn how to do it on their own. Then they will leave their parents’ nest for good. NBC Bay Area’s Kris Sanchez has more in the video report above.
